data.title: Market Voices: Ray Dalio, Amazon CEO, Novartis CEO on Trump tariffs
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • Bridgewater Associates founder Ray Dalio said President Trump's aggressive foreign policy tactics could end up scaring off investors from buying U.S. assets.

“On the other side of trade deficits and trade wars, there are capital and capital wars,” Dalio told CNBC at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. “If you take the conflicts, you can’t ignore the possibility of the capital wars. In other words, maybe there’s not the same inclination to buy U.S. debt and so on.”

Dalio added he is worried that countries with significant holdings of U.S. dollars and Treasurys will be less inclined to finance American deficits amid escalating geopolitical tensions.

“We know that both the holders of U.S. dollars are denominated … and those who need it, the United States, are worried about each other. Right? So if you have other countries who are holding it, and they’re worried about each other, and we’re producing a lot of it, that’s a big issue,” Dalio said. 

  • Amazon (AMZN) CEO Andy Jassy said higher U.S. tariffs are beginning to be reflected in the prices of certain goods sold through its website.

Jassy attributed the rise to merchants exhausting inventories pre-purchased ahead of the tariffs.

“So you start to see some of the tariffs creep into some of the prices, some of the items, and you see some sellers are deciding that they’re passing on those higher costs to consumers in the form of higher prices, some are deciding that they’ll absorb it to drive demand, and some are doing something in between,” Jassy told CNBC at the Davos summit. “I think you’re starting to see more of that impact."

  • Novartis (NVS) CEO Vas Narasimhan said he believes the Swiss company's trade deal with the Trump administration, which calls for Novartis to invest $23B in U.S. operations, will likely shield it from U.S. tariffs on pharmaceuticals.

“We expect to be in a position by middle of this year where we are not really exposed to tariffs because we’re able to produce in the U.S. for the U.S. We have inventory on hand,” Narasimhan told CNBC at Davos. 

“We also have an agreement with the U.S. government that excludes us from any tariffs, we think, but in case that were not to be the case, we’re also future-proofed in the other direction as well,” Narasimhan added.

data.title: Trump's ideal pick for Fed chair becomes more elusive - report
data.description:
It's becoming clear that no one candidate will tick all the boxes for President Donald Trump's wish list for the next Federal Reserve chair, according to a media report on Tuesday. Furthermore, the Department of Justice's probe into current Fed Chair Jerome Powell raises questions about the central bank's independence, making the matter more complicated.

Trump wants a loyalist who will push the Federal Open Market Committee to significantly cut interest rates, who appeals to both the MAGA base and Wall Street, and who can clear a Senate confirmation, in addition to looking the part, Bloomberg News reported. He has become “somewhat frustrated” with his choices, the article said, citing people familiar with the matter.

The list of finalists stands at National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, BlackRock's (BLK) Rick Rieder, current Fed Governor Chris Waller, and former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh. Hassett had been considered the frontrunner until last week. Trump mused that his chief economic adviser was too valuable as a spokesperson for the administration's economic agenda. 

Prediction markets recently have Warsh as the favorite, with a 56% chance, followed by Rieder at 21%, according to Kalshi.

Rieder's odds of getting the post increased since Trump's remarks about Hassett last week.  He may have an easier time getting Senate confirmation and handily gets Wall Street approval, but the MAGA base may oppose him as a “globalist” banker.  Waller has an advantage in already being through the confirmation for his current role on the Fed board, but his eagerness to pursue Trump's reform of the central bank may be questioned. Meanwhile, Warsh isn't a known MAGA personality and doesn't have a dovish track record, Bloomberg said.

In an interview on Fox Business last week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who's running the Fed chair search, said, "What I can tell you is he's been very deliberate.  He has asked exactly the questions that I think you and I and the markets would want asked — who can bring the board along with them?  Who has the gravitas?. You know, everything you would want done."

Bessent also added that the administration wants someone with an "open Greenspan-like mind" that, in a productivity boom like the '90s, wouldn't "just throw the brakes on things because they're alarmed by a high GDP number."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Rockwell Automation slides after Oppenheimer downgrade; Trane upgraded
data.description:
Shares of Rockwell Automation (ROK) fell as much as 3.3% Tuesday trading after analysts at Oppenheimer lowered their rating on the industrial automation firm to Perform from Outperform.

Analyst Noah Kaye said order trends are expected to improve only gradually in coming quarters, falling short of the stronger acceleration previously anticipated. He also noted that much of the near-term recovery appears reflected in the stock, which is trading close to historically elevated valuation levels.

In contrast, Oppenheimer upgraded Trane Technologies (TT) to Outperform from Perform and set a price target of $460. The firm cited a more attractive entry point following recent share price weakness, alongside supportive fourth-quarter booking comparisons and improving indicators in the non-residential market.

Trane (TT) shares were down roughly 1.4% Tuesday amid a broader selloff in U.S. stocks as investors pondered geopolitical tensions between the United States and Europe over Greenland.

data.title: Legend Biotech Arcellx rise after FDA draft guidance on multiple myeloma trials
data.description:
Arcellx (ACLX) and Legend Biotech (LEGN) traded higher on Tuesday after the U.S. FDA issued draft guidance on the use of minimal residual disease and complete response as study endpoints that can support accelerated approval of treatments for multiple myeloma, a rare blood cancer.

Legend (LEGN) has a partnership with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to market and develop Carvykti, a one-time cell therapy for relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma, while Arcellx (ACLX) is advancing its MM therapy, anito-cel, with Kite Pharma, a unit of Gilead Sciences (GILD).

In a research note, analysts from RBC Capital Markets viewed the FDA’s draft guidance positively. They noted that due to a requirement for large sample sizes, the regulator has suggested that the use of overall response rate ((ORR)) could be infeasible, and MRD negativity could be used as an acceptable surrogate for progression-free survival ((PFS)) and overall survival ((OS)) instead.

Pointing out that Legend’s (LEGN) CARTITUDE-6 trial for Carvykti had already been designed with MRD negativity as an endpoint, Jefferies thinks that the guidance is a “clear positive” for the company, on which it has an Outperform rating and a $66 per share target.

The analysts also pointed out that the update has competitive readthroughs with anito-cel from Gilead (GILD) and Arcellx (ACLX), expected to reach the market in the second-line to fourth-line settings based on MRD negativity.

“However, given we think LEGN shares are trading at a significant discount, this doesn’t necessarily change our bigger picture on the setup,” Jefferies wrote.

data.title: Ethos Technologies files preliminary prospectus for IPO of ~5.13M class A shares
data.description:
Ethos Technologies (LIFE) on Tuesday said it has filed a preliminary prospectus with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for an initial public offering of ~5.13M class A shares.

Currently, the IPO price per share is estimated at between $18.00 and $20.00.

The Austin-based insurance technology company said it has applied to list its class A shares on the Nasdaq Global Select Market, or Nasdaq, under the ticker symbol LIFE.

Furthermore, the company said "selling stockholders" are set to offer an additional ~5.4M class A shares.

Ethos said the information in the preliminary prospectus is not complete and may be changed.

data.title: 3M shares fall to 3-mth low as 2026 profit outlook comes up short
data.description:
Shares of 3M (MMM) fell as much as 8.8% to touch a three-month low Tuesday after the industrial conglomerate issued a 2026 earnings forecast that narrowly missed Wall Street expectations, highlighting the difficulty of sustaining its turnaround amid uneven economic conditions.

The company said adjusted earnings this year will range from $8.50 to $8.70 a share, with the midpoint slightly below the $8.61 consensus estimate tracked by S&P Global. Adjusted revenue is expected to grow roughly 4%.

The guidance suggests momentum may be moderating for Chief Executive Officer Bill Brown’s overhaul of the company, which has focused on boosting efficiency across its industrial footprint and reinvigorating product development. Those initiatives helped 3M (MMM) exceed analyst forecasts several times in 2025.

For the fourth quarter, 3M (MMM) reported adjusted earnings of $1.83 a share, edging past the $1.80 analysts had projected. Adjusted operating margin came in at 21.1%, just under expectations of 21.3%.

Economic conditions remain the largest variable for the company’s results, according to Bloomberg Intelligence analyst Mustafa Okur, who said the outlook points to steady progress toward medium-term goals but little evidence of a sharper acceleration or major portfolio shifts.

As of about 7:30 a.m. in New York, 3M shares were down roughly 4% ahead of the market open. The stock had risen more than 20% over the past year, outperforming the S&P 500’s roughly 17% gain.

Push toward data centers and nuclear

Brown has been steering 3M (MMM) toward end markets with stronger cyclical growth. The company’s electrical segment has been targeting data center customers, including cables and related accessories, as global investment in AI-driven infrastructure expands. Data center-related activity represents about 3% of total revenue, according to Bank of America analyst Andrew Obin.

Separately, 3M (MMM) is positioning its nuclear-related operations, which support applications such as fuel control and isotope separation, to benefit from new reactor construction as power demand rises.

Brown has also explored strategic options for the conglomerate, including discussions with advisers about potential asset sales within its industrial businesses, Bloomberg reported in October, as part of a broader effort to move away from slower-growing segments.

In addition, the company has been increasing its use of digital tools, including the development of an AI-powered assistant aimed at cutting prototyping costs and accelerating product development.

U.S. stocks were broadly under pressure Tuesday as investors reacted to geopolitical tensions between the United States and Europe over Greenland.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp outlines 4%–6% 2026 net revenue growth target as BTIG acquisition advances capital markets expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: U.S. Bancorp (USB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Gunjan Kedia, President, CEO & Director, reported "earnings per share of $1.26, an increase of approximately 18% year-over-year on an adjusted basis" and record net revenues of $7.4 billion for the quarter and $28.7 billion for the full year. She emphasized "meaningful positive operating leverage of 440 basis points as adjusted" and highlighted expense management, noting "four signature productivity programs have helped us deliver 9 straight quarters of largely stable expenses." Kedia announced the closing of the BTIG acquisition, describing it as a "bolt-on acquisition" after a long-standing partnership and expressed confidence in building "an extraordinary capital markets franchise." She also spotlighted strong fee growth in the Global Fund Services business, with GFS total net revenue growing at an 11% CAGR since 2021 and 12% in 2025. Kedia stated, "Our payments transformation is a strategic and long-term priority for the company," with improved growth rates and a focus on the small business segment.

  • John Stern, Vice Chair & Chief Financial Officer, stated, "we reported earnings per common share of $1.26 and achieved record net revenue of $7.4 billion this quarter." Stern highlighted improved credit quality metrics and an 18.2% increase in tangible book value per common share year-over-year. He reported a return on tangible common equity of 18.4% and an efficiency ratio of 57.4%. Stern noted, "total average deposits increased 0.7% linked quarter to $515 billion" and "average loans totaled $384 billion, up 1.4% from the prior quarter." Stern confirmed that "net interest income on a fully taxable equivalent basis totaled $4.3 billion, an increase of 1.4% on a linked-quarter basis." He emphasized that "net interest income, fee revenue and noninterest expense all exceeded our previous guidance." Stern provided guidance for 2026, expecting "total net revenue growth to be in the range of 4% to 6% compared to the prior year" and "positive operating leverage of 200 basis points or more for the full year." The BTIG acquisition is expected to contribute $175 million to $200 million of fee revenue per quarter.

Outlook

  • Management expects "total net revenue growth to be in the range of 4% to 6% compared to the prior year" for 2026, excluding the impact of the BTIG acquisition. The company aims to deliver "positive operating leverage of 200 basis points or more for the full year." For Q1 2026, guidance includes "net interest income growth...in the range of 3% to 4%" and "total fee revenue growth...in the range of 5% to 6% compared to the first quarter of 2025." Stern added, "noninterest expense growth of approximately 1% compared to the first quarter of 2025."

Financial Results

  • U.S. Bancorp delivered "earnings per share of $1.26," "record net revenue of $7.4 billion" for Q4, and "positive operating leverage of 440 basis points as adjusted." Fee revenue grew 7.6% year-over-year, with fee income representing 42% of total net revenues for the full year. Net interest income was reported at $4.3 billion for the quarter, and the net interest margin increased 2 basis points sequentially to 2.77%. Stern highlighted "noninterest expense totaled approximately $4.2 billion, up 0.7% linked quarter as FDIC expense favorability was partially offset by severance charges." Asset quality improved, with a net charge-off ratio of 0.54% and allowance for credit losses of $7.9 billion representing 2.03% of period-end loans. The common equity Tier 1 capital ratio was 10.8% or 9.3% including AOCI.

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler & Co.: Asked about share repurchase plans and capital ratios. Stern replied, "our intention is to grow our share repurchase amount starting this quarter in a gradual way. Will likely go from $100 million or so to $200 million and then the commitment to glide into our 75% payout target."
  • Siefers also inquired about credit card rate cap and the Credit Card Competition Act. Kedia responded, "Our estimate is that 90-plus percent of our clients will see a detrimental impact if there was an across-the-board 10% rate cap on credit cards" but noted recent discussions have focused on customer options.
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: Questioned revenue growth expectations and drivers. Stern indicated "mid-single-digit growth is appropriate for both net interest income and fee revenue growth" and expects momentum to continue. Pancari also asked about flexibility to achieve positive operating leverage. Kedia confirmed, "We have a lot of ability to flex that. Those are not long-term investments, things like sales and marketing are quarterly investments."
  • John McDonald, Truist: Sought details on balance sheet growth and loan mix. Stern projected "3% to 4% loan growth area led by commercial and card again" and reaffirmed confidence in deposit growth and NIM expansion.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: Asked about deposit mix and digital assets. Stern cited success with "Bank Smartly" and reduced CD usage, while Kedia described progress in digital assets and stablecoins, noting "the revenue model is still to be figured out" for payments applications.
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Asked about BTIG acquisition and buyback impact. Stern stated, "There's no impact to our buyback," while Kedia explained the strategic rationale and integration plans.
  • Erika Penala, UBS: Queried asset thresholds and liquidity rules. Stern confirmed growth strategies are not constrained by regulatory thresholds and that "there is no impact" from LCR changes on NIM guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to cautiously optimistic tone, focusing on capital deployment, regulatory risk, loan and deposit growth, fee income sustainability, and the BTIG acquisition. Probing questions surfaced around share repurchases, digital assets, and the impact of new regulations.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing momentum, flexibility, and readiness to adapt to changing conditions. Phrases such as "We have a high degree of confidence," "We are very committed," and "we expect stability" signaled assurance throughout the call. Compared to the previous quarter, management's outlook was more forward-leaning, bolstered by recent acquisitions and operating leverage achievements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 provided a more detailed revenue growth range of 4% to 6%, compared to the prior quarter's absence of formal 2026 guidance. The BTIG acquisition marked a significant strategic development. Management reiterated positive operating leverage as a priority but shifted from expense-driven to revenue-driven strategies for 2026. Fee revenue and net interest income growth were both called out as expected mid-single-digit contributors. Analysts' focus shifted from operational discipline and margin expansion to the integration of new businesses and strategic investments in technology and payments. Management tone was more confident and specific, reflecting greater clarity and momentum entering 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited regulatory and policy changes as primary risks, especially around capital requirements, credit card rate caps, and digital asset regulation. Kedia stated, "Our attention really is on unexpected policy changes. In front of us is a very big capital bill, in front of us are some real discussions around stablecoin...and novel charters."
  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of deposit growth, digital asset monetization, and the potential regulatory impact of asset growth and acquisitions. Management addressed these with assurances of operational flexibility, robust risk management, and prudent investment pacing.

Final Takeaway

U.S. Bancorp closed 2025 with strong financial momentum, record earnings, and a clear strategy for 2026 centered on driving organic growth and expanding its capital markets presence through the BTIG acquisition. Management expects net revenue growth of 4% to 6% in 2026, positive operating leverage of at least 200 basis points, and continued progress in payments and digital asset initiatives. The company remains vigilant regarding regulatory and policy shifts while emphasizing its operational flexibility, disciplined expense management, and sustained investments in technology and fee-generating businesses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cruise stocks caught in perfect storm of geopolitics, Wave Season promos
data.description:
Travel-related stocks are pinned down on Tuesday as heightened tensions between the EU and U.S. over Greenland fuel a risk-off trade and serve as a reminder of how quickly geopolitical tensions can erode risk appetite for travel-exposed names.

The move lower within the cruise sector was amplified by a pair of price target reductions for Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCHL) and Royal Caribbean (RCL) given the risk of increased promotional activity during “Wave Season.” 

“Our work points to a step-up in promotional intensity January-to-date, with Wave Season-related promotions beginning & ending at varying lengths for brands and at varying promotional rates/depth creating a more competitive landscape among brands to capture demand from consumers who may be ‘holding off’ to wait for the best deals,” Matthew Boss of J.P. Morgan said in a note to clients reflecting a -30% price target adjustment for Norwegian Cruise Lines (NCLH) to $28.

At the same time, Stifel’s Steven Wieczynski is “somewhat nervous” heading into Royal Caribbean’s (RCL) 2026 guidance scheduled for next week. While the analyst isn’t worried about RCL’s business or that demand is fading, rather that investor expectations for how RCL will initially guide for 2026 is “aggressive/backwards.”

“It wouldn’t surprise us if RCL’s initial guidance underwhelms and the shares experience a near-term correction,” Wieczynski says, lowering his price target by 5% to $380.

Consequently, the backdrop continues to weigh on the cruise sector, deepening a multi-day sell-off.

Norwegian Cruise Lines (NCLH), Royal Caribbean (RCL) and Carnival Corp (CCL) are all down for six consecutive days, while Viking Holdings (VIK) is in the red for a fifth straight day.

data.title: Ford shares set to trade in red for seven straight sessions
data.description:
Ford (F) shares set to extend losses for seven straight sessions, as the stock is down 1.80% at $13.36 during trading hours on Tuesday. 

The company lost nearly 4.25% over the preceding six sessions. Ford closed 1.52% lower on Monday at $13.60.

According to sources cited by The Wall Street Journal, Ford (F) is turning to China’s number one automaker for batteries to supply its hybrid vehicle production and replace terminated agreements with LG Energy Solutions and SK On.

The two companies are reportedly in discussions in which Ford would buy batteries manufactured by BYD (BYDDY) (BYDDF) for its hybrid vehicles. Under one discussed scenario, Ford would import BYD (BYDDY) (BYDDF) batteries to Ford’s overseas factories.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, Ford has a Hold rating with a score of 3.34 out of 5. The company received an A+ in the prospect of profitability, while it got an F for growth and a C for revisions.

Turning to the Wall Street community, four out of 21 analysts gave Ford a Buy or above, 16 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and one recommended Strong Sell.

However, Seeking Alpha analysts are bullish and view the stock as a Buy.

Seeking Alpha analyst Dilantha De Silva rated the stock Buy, saying that despite delayed EV profitability targets (now 2029) and the loss of the EV growth premium, shares remain attractive at 12.8x forward earnings for investors willing to embrace the new-look Ford.

Overall, the stock has gained nearly 30% over the past year, compared to the approximately 17% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: More than half of Instagram's ads were in Reels format in 2025—Sensor Tower
data.description:
Advertisements shown via Reels rose 35% in 2025 on Meta's Instagram (META) and made up more than half of all the ads shown on the platform, according to data from Sensor Tower.

Watching Reels made up 46% of time spent on Instagram in the U.S. last year, up from 37% in 2024. On Facebook, it rose 29%.

Sensor Tower data showed that the rise in Reels viewership contributed to a jump in overall activity on Meta's photo-sharing app. Daily active users on Instagram rose 2% from last year, helped by increased usage of Reels.

The analytics firm's data revealed that watch time for Shorts on YouTube (GOOG) (GOOGL) was flat last year but was still ahead of rivals Instagram and TikTok on mobile. Sensor Tower estimated the number of U.S. daily active users on YouTube grew 3% in 2025.

TikTok (BDNCE), however, leads in time spent per day on the app, with users averaging 81 minutes, compared with 80 minutes on YouTube and 55 minutes on Instagram.
